titleStmt.title: Karantina – MA (M.71) – Birth; Civil War (1)
WIBARAB Corpus
titleStmt.funder: Funded by the European Research Council under the Grant Agreement 101020127

publicationStmt.distributor: Austrian Center for Digital Humanities and Cultural Heritage

recording.p: Recorded during Lebanon Fieldwork Campaign Ana Iriarte Diez 2022

prefixDef.p: Private URIs using the teiCorpusHeader prefix are pointers to any element in the WIBARAB teiCorpus document.
Private URIs using the share prefix are pointers to audio file residing on the WIBARAB network share.
Private URIs using the geo prefix are pointers to the xml:id attribute on a place element in VICAV Geodata list.
Private URIs using the dmp prefix are pointers to the xml:id attribute on an element in the WIBARAB DMP document..
Private URIs using the zotid prefix are pointers to the xml:id attribute on a biblStruct element in the TEI export of the VICAV Zotero Group library.
Private URIs using the sources prefix are pointers to the xml:id attribute on an element in the WIBARAB list of sources.

spanGrp.span: Were you born here in Karantina?
Me? Maybe here. When I was born in ... I was registered in (lit. my ID was from) Burj el-Shemali, in Tyre.
No! We moved our registry (lit. our identities) from there to here, we moved them in 1972.
But during the civil war and the massacre you were here in the area, right?
No! During the massacre, yes! During the massacre ... During the civil war (I was) here.
The civil war was, it was ... First of all it was all a big lie. A big lie it was!
It was not about Palestinians and not ... It became sectarian.
As for me (P1s), the first one, Y, my son, was born here, he was a month old.
When we left the area he was a month old.
And the bombing, how was it like? "The sea lies before you and the enemy lies behind you and the sea is turbulent"
And you cannot escape by the sea! Did you see Abu Shadya's pictures on the TV, my son?
As he says, may God never repeat again (lit. may God not show us) those days.

We walked barefoot, excuse the expression. We would step all over the dead bodies (lit. the killed ones). NOTE أَجَلَّك / أَجَلِّك ‎[P] excuse my mentioning it, excuse the expression (literally: I hope that God will honor you. The expression is used when one mentions something that is unpleasant or rude); { ʔajallak / ʔajallek}
All over the dead bodies we had to step!
I would be carrying my son and I didn't have any milk and he would cry.
Yussef didn't have ... He was one month old, my son. He was born in 1976, and we left ... He was born in December, and on January 19th we left and migrated.
We wanted to leave the building but couldn't. They started (coming) from the roof flying with helicopters and by sea ... Yes!
If it weren't for this young ... !
The mills ... This mill (here), we were sieged in it. *****
They then broke the wall.
Yes, we were sieged inside, true.
Who tore it down? The son of Daww, from the Daww family.
But for three days the siege remained...? **********
Yes, all of us, all the ʕArab community.
and whoever would go out would get shot.
he would get shot
Look, they left a road, it went from the beginning of the bridge under the tunnel.
We passed and crossed to the Armenian's (neighborhood). The Armenians received us... They gave us...
Yes they welcomed us, poor us!
The kids would welcome ...
We would wear clothes ... We were wearing the old clothes, I mean.
We had (our own) clothes, I mean!
Here, the matresses were full of gold, they called it here "The Gold Area"
They would hide in the matresses, the bracelets, the gold and the money... I mean, coins!
I left to my brother in law, may he rest in peace...
Come, get in! (Addressing a little girl that stood by the door)
Nothing, there are people (inside), you can say hello to them now.
Come in, my grandchild! This is Y's daughter.
